questions.question: What type of user accounts must be used to enable Notion Calendar for a team?

options.text: Personal Notion accounts
options.explanation: Personal Notion accounts are not sufficient; the feature requires Google Workspace‑managed accounts.

options.text: Google Workspace‑managed accounts
options.explanation: Correct – the article states that team members must be managed by Google Workspace to use Notion Calendar.

options.text: Microsoft 365 accounts
options.explanation: Microsoft 365 accounts are unrelated to the requirement described.

options.text: Apple ID accounts
options.explanation: Apple IDs are not mentioned as a requirement for Notion Calendar.

questions.question: After viewing a teammate’s calendar, how can you close that view?

options.text: Click the "Close" button at the top of the page
options.explanation: There is no dedicated "Close" button mentioned in the article.

options.text: Press Esc or click the x next to their calendar in the left sidebar
options.explanation: The article instructs to press Esc or use the x icon to exit the teammate’s calendar view.

options.text: Refresh the browser window
options.explanation: Refreshing would reload the page but does not specifically close the calendar view.

options.text: Log out of Notion and log back in
options.explanation: Logging out is unnecessary and unrelated to closing a calendar view.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly describes how to pin a teammate’s calendar to your own calendar list?

options.text: First view the teammate’s calendar, then right‑click their email in the left sidebar and choose “Pin to calendar list”.
options.explanation: Exactly as described: you must see the calendar, then right‑click the email and select Pin.

options.text: Drag the teammate’s email directly onto the calendar grid and select “Pin”.
options.explanation: Dragging creates a meeting; pinning is done via right‑click, not drag‑and‑drop.

options.text: Select “Show teammate calendar…”, then click a Pin icon that appears on the calendar view.
options.explanation: There is no Pin icon in the calendar view; pinning is done from the sidebar.

options.text: Click the x next to the teammate’s calendar and then choose “Pin”.
options.explanation: Clicking x removes the view; it does not lead to a pin option.

questions.question: If a Google Workspace admin wants to restrict team access to Notion Calendar, which resource should they consult?

options.text: The main Notion Calendar for Teams article
options.explanation: That article explains usage, not admin security controls.

options.text: The Notion Calendar security practices article linked in the page
options.explanation: The article explicitly points admins to the security practices article for restriction guidance.

options.text: The Google Workspace admin console documentation
options.explanation: While useful, the Notion Help Center directs admins to its own security practices article.

options.text: The Notion Help Center FAQ page
options.explanation: The FAQ is generic and does not specifically address admin restrictions.

questions.question: When you drag a teammate from the Notion Calendar menu onto the grid to schedule a 1:1 meeting, which of the following items is NOT automatically populated?

options.text: Meeting title
options.explanation: The title is auto‑filled according to the article.

options.text: Meeting time
options.explanation: The time is also auto‑filled.

options.text: Participant
options.explanation: The teammate you dragged becomes the participant automatically.

options.text: Meeting location
options.explanation: Location is not mentioned as an automatically added field; you would need to set it manually if needed.

questions.question: What is the effect of selecting “Remove teammate from list” after right‑clicking a teammate’s calendar?

options.text: The teammate’s calendar is permanently deleted from Notion
options.explanation: Removal only affects your view; the calendar remains in Notion.

options.text: The calendar is removed from your sidebar list but remains available in Notion
options.explanation: Correct – the action only hides the calendar from your list.

options.text: The teammate loses access to Notion entirely
options.explanation: Access rights are unrelated to this UI action.

options.text: All events on that calendar are permanently erased
options.explanation: Events are retained; only the list entry is removed.

questions.question: Why must you click the “•••” (ellipsis) next to your work calendar before selecting “Show teammate calendar…”?

options.text: It opens a contextual menu where the “Show teammate calendar…” option resides.
options.explanation: The ellipsis reveals the menu containing the option to view a teammate’s calendar.

options.text: It synchronizes your work calendar with Google Calendar before showing others.
options.explanation: Synchronization is not mentioned as a prerequisite for this action.

options.text: It creates a new calendar for the teammate automatically.
options.explanation: The action only displays an existing calendar; it does not create one.

options.text: It logs you out of Notion and then logs you back in with elevated permissions.
options.explanation: Logging out is unrelated to accessing a teammate’s calendar.

questions.question: Which statement best describes how conferencing integration works with the drag‑and‑drop 1:1 meeting feature?

options.text: Conferencing must be added manually after the meeting is created.
options.explanation: The article says conferencing is added automatically.

options.text: Conferencing is automatically added based on your preferences when you drag a teammate onto the grid.
options.explanation: Correct – the feature auto‑populates conferencing according to your saved preferences.

options.text: Conferencing cannot be used with drag‑and‑drop meetings; you need to schedule them elsewhere.
options.explanation: The article explicitly mentions conferencing is added automatically for these meetings.

options.text: Conferencing is only available for group meetings, not 1:1 meetings.
options.explanation: The feature works for 1:1 meetings as described.
